smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: made these for you
thread.body_text:
hi Ellie, spent a few minutes on the Twisted Pepper site and I'm a little annoyed we're not working together yet haha.

but that's not why I reached out. I handle Meta ads for ecom brands, and instead of sending a BS pitch, I built you some.

built 3 ad concepts from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus a world-class brand playbook I pulled from your site and customer reviews. it's all done and waiting on my side.

should I send it over?

- Kyle

thread.subject: RE: made these for you
thread.body_text:
Ellie - the thing most brands notice first isn't the ads, it's the brand playbook. it maps your voice, the exact words your customers use in reviews, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

it's ready to go with the mockups.

 want me to pass both along?

- Kyle
please send me the brand playbook please

here you go Ellie - 3 ad mockups built from Twisted Pepper's own products, plus your brand playbook .

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

copy.tldr: Twisted Pepper is a small handcrafted dip, soup, rub and seasoning brand with great taste and loyal fans but low prices and no ads running, so the whole call is about proving one product can sell at a profit before scaling.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A handcrafted gourmet food brand founded by Ellie Hughes in 2005. They sell dip mixes, soup mixes, BBQ rubs and seasonings direct to consumers online, plus wholesale and fundraisers. Based in Wichita, Kansas.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: 103 products from $5.50 to $48.99, with a median price of $9.99. That median is our stand-in for their average order value since we cannot see their real order data. Single mixes are cheap ($6 to $13). The bigger tickets are 4-packs ($22.99), 3-packs ($29 to $36) and gift boxes.
deep_dive.positioning: Approachable gourmet. Fine-restaurant flavor made fast and easy for busy home cooks. Gluten free, no added MSG, low sodium. Not mass-market bland.
deep_dive.who_buys: Busy home cooks who want bold flavor without the work, and gift-givers buying for people who love food. Holiday and gifting looks like a real seasonal driver based on their gift boxes and one testimonial.
customer_voice: I am absolutely addicted to your soups and dips/cheese ball mixes. To be honest - all those I bought from you never went for gifts! They went in our stomachs, and to share with extended family and friends over the holidays.
Jalapeno Lime Rub is absolutely amazing. I use in avacado toast, deviled eggs, steak and chicken.
My husband Loves the Pig Sweat Rub. He sprinkles on his bacon while cooking, and anything pork! I order 4 jars at time.
strengths: Real, specific customer love. The quotes name exact products and exact uses (bacon, avocado toast, deviled eggs). That is gold for ad copy.
Clear point of difference that is easy to say in an ad: gourmet restaurant flavor made fast and easy, gluten free, low sodium, no added MSG.
gaps: Very few reviews visible on the site, and two testimonials are still placeholder text ('Author's name, Los Angeles, CA'). Almost no social proof to lean on in ads.
Their single products are cheap ($6 to $13). At those prices a single-item sale will struggle to cover ad costs. We have to push bundles and free-shipping-over-$40 to make the math work.

game_plan.headline: Start with the ads on their Scroll-Stopper Sheet, prove one bestseller can sell at a profit, then push bundles and the $40 free-shipping threshold to lift order value.

phases.goal: Prove that paid ads can turn a profit on at least one product. We are starting from zero, so month one is about learning, not scale.
campaigns: One sales campaign, cold audience, broad targeting so Meta can find the buyers.
One retargeting campaign for people who visit the site but do not buy.
lead_products: Lead with the BBQ Rubs, specifically the Jalapeno Lime Rub. We think this is their strongest single product because a customer wrote a detailed, specific rave about it (avocado toast, deviled eggs, steak, chicken) and another named the Pig Sweat Rub as a repeat buy. Those reviews are the best social proof they have. We would run the first ad from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet that features the rubs first.
Also test one dip 4-pack ($22.99), like the Dipping Madness or Veggies & Chiles pack, because the higher price makes the ad math easier than a $6 single mix.
phases.creative: Run all 3 ads from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet we already sent. Start with the rub ad since the rub has the most believable customer proof. Pair the copy with the real customer quotes we found. Keep the offer simple: free shipping when you spend $40, plus a free gift.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We find at least one ad and one product combination that brings in sales at a cost we can live with. We are looking for the winner, not big volume yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen the audience.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and product from Phase 1 with a higher daily budget.
Add one or two new audiences and one or two new ad angles built around the winner.
lead_products: Whatever won in Phase 1. If the Jalapeno Lime Rub led, double down and add a second rub angle. If a dip 4-pack led, push the other 4-packs too.
phases.creative: Build 2 to 3 fresh ads off the winning angle. Lean harder on the free-shipping-over-$40 offer to nudge people toward bundles and lift the average order size.
phases.budget: Step up gradually, only as far as the results allow. No fixed number promised.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winner still holds up at higher spend. Order value creeps up as more people hit the $40 free-shipping mark.

phases.goal: Scale what is working and add a second product line so we are not resting on one item.
campaigns: Launch a second product line as a new test (for example the Soup Collections 3-packs at $30, which carry a healthier price than single mixes).
If we are approaching the fall gifting season, start warming up the Gift Boxes and Gift Bags.
lead_products: Second line: the Soup Collection 3-packs ($30 each: Home Cookin', South of the Border, Comfort). Higher ticket than singles, and 'Soup's On in 30 minutes' is an easy hook.
Gift Boxes for early holiday gift shoppers, given one customer already treats these as gifts.
phases.creative: New ads for the second product line, plus gifting-angle ads if the calendar lines up. Keep the proven winner running the whole time.
phases.budget: Scale in line with results. Still no guaranteed number.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two product lines both selling at a profit, and a bigger, steadier flow of orders than day one.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get a small but steady flow of clicks each day in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and product actually sell. We do not know their margin yet, so we would confirm this number on the call.

estimates.label: Order value we are working with
estimates.value: Around $10 per order as a starting guess
estimates.basis: Their median product price is $9.99, and we used that as a stand-in because we cannot see their real order data. This is a big reason to push bundles and the $40 free-shipping offer, which should lift the real number well above $10.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a dollar figure on this yet
estimates.basis: Honest reason: we have no view of their current sales, margin, or repeat-buy rate. Any number would be a guess. We would set a real target once we know their margin and their true average order value (see discovery).

assumptions: The Jalapeno Lime Rub is one of their stronger sellers. We think so because it has the most detailed positive review, but we cannot confirm without their sales data.
Their real average order value is higher than $10 once bundles and the $40 free-shipping offer kick in. If most orders are single $6 to $10 items, the ad math gets hard and the plan changes.
Their margin on mixes is healthy enough to fund ads. Dry mixes usually carry good margin, but we need Ellie to confirm.
The checkout and site work fine and can handle new traffic.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We could not find any Meta ads live. Confirm they are truly starting from zero. If they tried before and stopped, find out why.

questions.question: Who handles your marketing today, you or someone on your team?
questions.listening_for: Is Ellie doing everything herself? A solo founder means simpler decisions but less time and budget. Sets expectations on pace.

questions.question: Have you ever tried Meta ads before? If so, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Past pain. If a previous try flopped, learn the reason. It tells us if the problem was creative, offer, or the product price being too low.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship a typical mix, so we know what room we have to spend on ads?
questions.listening_for: Their margin. This decides if $6 to $13 singles can ever carry ads, or if we must live in bundles. Do not push for exact revenue, just the cost side.

questions.question: What does a typical order look like - one item, or do people usually buy a few?
questions.listening_for: Their real average order value. This is the single biggest unknown in the plan. If most orders clear $40, the math is easy. If it is one $6 mix, we have a problem to solve first.

questions.question: Which products actually sell the best for you right now?
questions.listening_for: Confirms or corrects our guess that the rubs and Jalapeno Lime lead. Whatever she names becomes the first ad we scale. This can change the whole Phase 1 pick.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site for the first time, do they usually buy, or mostly leave?
questions.listening_for: A sense of whether the site converts. If new visitors rarely buy, the problem is the site or offer, not the ads, and we fix that first.

questions.question: I noticed the site has very few reviews showing, and a couple look like placeholder text. Do you have more reviews sitting somewhere, like email or Facebook?
questions.listening_for: Hidden social proof we can put into ads and onto the site. Ads with real reviews sell far better. If she has none, we need a plan to gather them.

questions.question: How much of your business is gifting versus people buying for themselves?
questions.listening_for: If gifting is big, we time gift boxes to the fall and holidays. Shapes Phase 3 heavily.

questions.question: Do people come back and reorder, or is it mostly one-time buyers?
questions.listening_for: Repeat buying makes cheap products viable, because the first sale does not have to pay for everything. High reorder rate changes what we can afford to spend.

questions.question: Do you get customers who found you by searching Google for things like 'gluten free dip mix' or 'BBQ rub'?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for BBQ rubs, dip mixes and seasonings, so there is a real Google opening here. Listen for whether they already get any search traffic. If yes, Google Shopping is a natural next step after Meta proves out.

questions.question: How much of your time goes to the wholesale and fundraiser side versus online sales?
questions.listening_for: Where her focus is. If wholesale is the real engine, online ads may be a smaller play, and we scope accordingly.

objections.objection: My products are cheap, how can ads possibly pay off on a $6 mix?
objections.answer: You are right that a single $6 mix will not carry ads on its own. That is exactly why we lead with your bundles and your $40 free-shipping offer, so each order is worth more. We also lean on repeat buyers, since your fans clearly reorder. The first sale does not have to pay for everything.

objections.objection: I have never run ads and I do not want to burn money finding out they do not work.
objections.answer: Fair. That is why month one starts small, around $50 a day, and the only goal is to find one ad and one product that turn a profit. We are not chasing scale until something proves it works. If nothing works at that small budget, you have not lost much and we both know quickly.

objections.objection: I do not really have reviews to show. Will the ads still work?
objections.answer: Thin social proof is the biggest thing holding your ads back, and it is fixable. You already have real fans who left detailed raves, we found them on your site. We would put those exact words into the ads and start collecting more reviews from every new order, so the proof builds while the ads run.

objections.objection: Why should I trust a projection when you have not seen my numbers?
objections.answer: I would not trust one either, which is why I am not giving you a made-up revenue number today. I would rather ask you two things - your cost to make a mix and what a typical order size is - and then set a real target together. Everything I have shown you comes from your own site and reviews.

red_flags.flag: Low prices and a likely low average order value. Median product price is $9.99 and many singles are $6 to $13.
red_flags.why_it_matters: At those prices a one-item sale may not cover the cost of the ad that made it. If most real orders are single cheap items, paid ads will struggle. We must confirm true order value and push bundles.

red_flags.flag: Almost no visible social proof. Few reviews, and two testimonials are still placeholder text ('Author's name, Los Angeles, CA').
red_flags.why_it_matters: Ads for food brands live or die on reviews. Cold buyers need proof it tastes good before they trust a small brand. We have little to lean on until we gather more.

red_flags.flag: Shipping policy page says 'Coming Soon' and is empty.
red_flags.why_it_matters: New buyers want to know shipping cost and speed before they buy. A blank shipping page can kill sales from ad traffic. Worth flagging to Ellie to fix early.

red_flags.flag: No ads currently running and no ad history we can find.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Not bad, but it means we start cold with no past data to learn from. Everything in month one is a first test, so we should not promise fast results.

other_channel_openings: Google Shopping and Search. People actively search for 'BBQ rub', 'gluten free dip mix' and 'soup mix', so there is real buyer demand to capture. This is a natural add-on once Meta proves which products sell, not a day-one move.

Basic SEO on product pages. Their product descriptions are rich but titles and pages could be tuned for the terms people search. A slower play, worth raising only if she asks about long-term free traffic.
